scriptText:
RTS - POWERADE

Client: Brian Ford, Kristen Rumble, Steve Powers
Date: April 7, 2026
Version: 1 
TOS
TRT
VISUAL
POWER IS...
0:00
A black screen. A single pulse of blue light. The iconic Powerade "P" logo flashes for a millisecond.
...NOT GIVEN.
0:02
A montage of every type of athlete, breath hitting cold air, hands chalking up, sneakers squeaking.
IT’S EARNED.
0:04
A quick-cut sequence of the full Powerade lineup. Blue, Red, and the New Purple.
ACTIVATE! 
0:06
Transition into the 3 bets - music transition here
THE FAST START: 
0:08
Close-up of the New Grape Power Water. Clear liquid, purple label. Fitness creators explode I’ll into action. (not Callie)
NEW GRAPE POWER WATER. 
0:10
High-speed macro shots of water droplets and the Grape flavor reveal.
FUEL THE FLOW 

Transition to Bet 2
FEEL THE MOMENTUM
0:13
A fitness creator’s sprint "match-cuts" into a pro soccer player charging down the pitch.  FIFA WWC visuals.
DURING THE  SUMMER OF SOCCER  
0:16
Sizzle footage of the World Cup stadium lights and the roar of the crowd. Pure soccer energy.
Young girls in HS jerseys, eyes locked on the goal. The future of the game. (0:11 and 0:14 here)
THEN, BACK TO SCHOOL…
0:22
Jesser catching a ball for transition.  He’s mid-laugh, mid-play, drinking a classic Powerade. (some product in this video)
WITH JESSER.
0:25
Jesser’s high-energy content. Real, raw, style. 
STAY HYDRATED.
0:28
Montage of Grape Water, Soccer, and Jesser.  The "Powerade Universe" in a blur.
EARN IT.   POWERADE. 
0:30
Powerade Logo + FIFA Partner Lockup.

SUPER:  BODYARMOR logo
SUPER:  IN 2027, OUR GOAL IS SIMPLE.
VISUALS:  ATHLETES IN ACTION, DRINK SHOTS 
SUPER:  DRIVE AWARENESS AND DEMAND
VISUALS:  ATHLETES IN ACTION, DRINK SHOTS 
SUPER:  CONVERT THAT DEMAND IN-STORE
VISUALS:  SHOPPERS IN STORE, REGULAR ATHLETES
SUPER:  AND BUILD OUT OUR CHOOSE BETTER CAMPAIGN  
VISUALS:  CHOOSE BETTER ATHLETE AND DRINK SHOTS AND ENDCARDS 
SUPER:  FIRST, WE’LL BUILD CULTURE RELEVANCE
VISUALS:  MARCH MADNESS, NHL, NASCAR, BARSTOOL SPORTS LOGOS AND IMAGERY 
SUPER:  WE’LL LEVERAGE LTOS ACROSS MASSIVE PLATFORMS
VISUALS:  SAVANNA BANANAS, NASCAR, DUDE PERFECT LOGOS AND IMAGERY
SUPER:  SECOND, WE’LL ACCELERATE GROWTH ACROSS THREE PLATFORMS
SUPER:  BASE IS BACK!  VELOCITY AND SHARE UP VS YEAR AGO 
VISUALS:  BODYARMOR BASE DRINK SHOTS 
SUPER: LYTE IS UP DOUBLE DIGITS AND BRINGING NEW FLAVORS
VISUALS:  NEW LYTE FLAVORS IMAGERY 
SUPER:  FLASH IV GOES BIG! 
VISUALS:  FLASH IV DRINK SHOTS, HARD WORKERS FROM SPOT, BLANEY DRINK
SUPER:  POWERED BY MARCH MADNESS, NASCAR AND IMMUNITY SEASON
VISUALS:  MM, NASCAR, HARDWORKERS “ON THEIR FEET” IMAGERY 
SUPER:  AND A FULL YEAR OF BODYARMOR FIT! 
SUPER:  ACTIVATING SORORITIES, INFLUENCERS AND CREATORS
VISUALS:  SORORITIES, INFLUENCERS AND CREATORS 
SUPER:  (BRING UP ONE AT A TIME)  BIG CULTURE. MODERN SPORT. REAL CONVERSION. 
SUPER:  FINALLY, WE’LL CREATE A NEW CAMPAIGN
VISUALS:  CHOOSE BETTER SHOTS AND END CARDS FROM SPOT.  
SUPER:  BEHIND THE STRATEGY OF “WE CHOOSE BETTER.” (have each word come up one at a time on WE CHOOSE BETTER) 
SUPER:  IN 2027, THAT’S HOW WE’LL DRIVE DEMAND, AND WIN IN STORE.  
VISUALS:  ATHLETES IN ACTION, DRINK SHOTS, ETC. 
SUPER:  LET’S GO!!!

scriptText:
Script Outline V2

SECTION ONE: DEFINING OUR PAST
Purpose: set the groundwork for why the evolution makes sense

SUPER:	BODYARMOR was created to be better than  		the neon sports drink defining the category.
VIS		(montage of sickly neon liquid and blurred out Gatorade symbols on sports 
imagery)
 		Better hydration. Better recovery. Better performance. 
(Retro but still sexy images of bottle, natural ingredients, product creation - 
montage)

Led by an athlete known for being…  	Better.
(Early days of images with Kobe and BODYARMOR)



SECTION TWO: CELEBRATING THE PRESENT
Purpose: Get credit for the first stage of the evolution and the results of it

SUPER	Last year, we rallied around better.
VIS		(CHOOSE BETTER campaign)

A new look. A new voice. A new era for the brand.
		(Montage of all this stuff)

More products. More opportunities.
		(Show FIT and Flash IV, show March Madness)

More people choosing BODYARMOR.
		(Text: “↑ Household Penetration”, “↑ Sales”, “↑ Spontaneous Awareness”)

Proving better is better for more people.
(overlayed over montage of athletes and regular people drinking BodyArmor 
SKUs)



SECTION THREE: CHARTING A COURSE FOR THE FUTURE
Purpose: Show how the brand evolution will continue to grow and bring in new consumers

SUPER	Now, we’re turning that momentum into something bigger
VIS		(Athlete cheering over victory)

		And building our challenge…
(Text: “CHOOSE BETTER” underneath super)
	(Bottle Macro shot lock up)
	
Into a declaration.
(Text: “WE CHOOSE BETTER” underneath super)
		(the “we” animates as the words shift over)
 		WE CHOOSE BETTER

WE CHOOSE BETTER when we formulate our products
(Behind-the-scenes formulation, liquid mixing, lab precision, ingredient 
sourcing. )

WE CHOOSE BETTER when we fuel our bodies with natural ingredients
(Athlete drinking post-workout, Parent handing bottle to kid, Close-up consumption shots, March Madness clips)

WE CHOOSE BETTER in this very room, together.
(Previous RTS meetings, Team huddles, Community sports, Diverse athletes/ 
real people, BodyArmor around)

		In 2027, BODYARMOR  		shows up louder.
(Big, noisy moments—crowds cheering, sideline coolers, splashes, high) energy.

Bolder.
(Logo in real-world environment (stadium signage, billboard, digital takeover)

More undeniable than ever.
(Rapid-fire montage of wins, scale, presence: Multiple sports, Multiple environments, Shelf dominance, Cultural moments)

And to prove to the world:
(Climatic montage of Athletes, multiple people drinking BodyArmor)

WE CHOOSE BETTER

scriptText:
COCA-COLA OSM – “DAY IN THE LIFE”

Client: Melanie Ostis
Writer: Matthew Montemaro
Date: 3/31/25
Version: 1

“DAY IN THE LIFE OF A CONSUMER”

AUDIO
VISUALS

(SFX)
MORNING COMMUTE SOUNDS

CONSUMER: 
VOICEOVER: Every day, Coca-Cola is part of life’s moments – big and small.


A city street. A consumer drives to work, passing a large Coca-Cola billboard with an ice-cold Coke Zero Sugar. 

A quick cut to a bus stop ad featuring a Coca-Cola campaign. 

(SFX) 
GAS PUMP BEEPING 

CONSUMER: 
VOICEOVER: Whether you’re on the move…


The consumer stops for gas. A digital ad on the pump screen plays a Coca-Cola commercial. 

The store window is filled with activation materials showcasing Coca-Cola brands.


(SFX) 
PHONE NOTIFICATION DINGS

CONSUMER: 
VOICEOVER: …or winding down after a long day, our brands are right there with you.


The consumer receives a delivery notification. 


A DoorDash driver hands them a takeout order, complete with a 20oz Coke.
(SFX) 
CAR DOOR CLOSES, RAIN PATTERING


CONSUMER: 
VOICEOVER: Convenient, refreshing, always within reach.

The consumer picks up a Click & Collect order at Walmart from the parking lot.



A Coca-Cola pack is visible entering their trunk as the customer unloads groceries.
(SFX)
KEYBOARD TYPING, COMPUTER MOUSE CLICKING

CONSUMER: 
VOICEOVER: From daily routines to special moments, Coca-Cola brings people together.


The consumer browses online, planning their next trip. 

A banner ad highlights Coca-Cola’s presence at theme parks, restaurants, and hotels. 
(Ad from Delta banner)

(SFX) 
FRIENDS LAUGHING, CLINKING GLASSES

CONSUMER: 
VOICEOVER: Because wherever life takes you, we’ll be there.

Footage from Publix/PB



A backyard gathering. Friends and family share a meal, raising their Cokes in a toast.

Beauty shot of plated burger paired with a crispy Coca-Cola.


(SFX) 
Music Jingle Outro

CUT TO: Coca-Cola logo, tagline: “Refreshing Every Moment.”

scriptText:
COCA-COLA – “PUBLIX DROID ADVENTURE”

Client: Michael Pelletier 
Writer: Matthew Montemaro
Date: 4/25/25
Version: 2
“Publix Droid Adventure”
Concept: Coca-Cola and Disney bring "Real Magic" to Publix shoppers through an immersive Star Wars-themed experience featuring an interactive Coca-Cola Droid.

AUDIO
VISUALS

(SFX)
Droid lights up subtly; sounds start.
Futuristic hums, soft droid beeps.














NARRATOR VO:
"What if every trip to Publix could feel like stepping into a galaxy far, far away?"

Publix storefront with Coca-Cola Star Wars Droid Display.
We see families entering the store.


GFX: Text overlay: “Coca-Cola and Disney bring Real Magic to Publix.”
 

(SFX) 
Happy droid chirps





NARRATOR VO
"Now greeting shoppers at the front of every Publix... a one-of-a-kind Droid display that lights up, interacts, and engages."

Emma walks inside with a Publix basket. 
Droid greets her with lights and sound. (CU of droid waving)

Wide shot reveals other guests scanning the QR code, kids posing for photos, and others grabbing 12-packs.



NARRATOR VO:
"Shoppers can record and send custom holograms, creating magical Star Wars moments to share with friends and family.”







(SFX) 

Sci-fi hologram buzz.



Emma taps the screen and records: "Hey guys, we’re all going to Galaxy’s Edge!"

(GFX) 
Shimmering blue projection effect of hologram.


(SFX) 
Celebratory ding; subtle Star Wars-themed score.







NARRATOR VO:
"With each visit, shoppers can unlock exclusive ticket packages to Disney Parks... including Galaxy’s Edge."


Screen shows Exclusive Disney Parks Offers in front of Emma in store from the hologram.


Screen animates to display ticket deals. Emma taps Buy Tickets. Emma smiles. 
We see Galaxy’s Edge assets on screen.

 

NARRATOR VO:
"An unforgettable experience that delights shoppers, drives traffic, and builds excitement — "It starts with a trip to Publix... and ends in a galaxy far, far away.”





(SFX)
Lightsaber swoosh, crowd cheer.

Show user experience:
Emma smiling in store, sips a Coca-Cola, as we transition to Emma and three friends now at Galaxy’s Edge — laughing, dressed in full Star Wars attire.

NARRATOR VO:
"For store managers, this activation drives front-of-store engagement, merchandise lift, and repeat traffic thanks to a fun digital treasure hunt experience." 






NARRATOR VO:
"Coca-Cola and Disney invite every shopper to experience the Real Magic. Only at Publix."









SFX: Star Wars orchestral finale.

Transition back to store:
Store manager enters back at Publix, proudly watches guests interact with Droid. Visuals of 12-pack lift and QR scans.


Infographic shows opportunities for store managers.


As we exit, Publix feels lively; kids wave at the Droid; we see joyful shopper moments.


GFX: Coca-Cola + Disney + Publix logos fade up with CTA, "Drive front-of-store excitement. Activate your Droid display today." 
Optional: QR code for store managers to access materials.

teachingPoints: AI heritage scripts need restraint, a clear visual thesis, style-lock discipline, and careful historical-claim boundaries.
Cinematic teasers should create a felt thesis through visual transformation, not explain the assignment in deck language.
Partnership scripts need both sides to feel intentional, with the shared audience or cultural reason made visible.
Platform explainers need a human friction first, then a clean system reveal and proof of usefulness.
Supers should carry argument beats, not label the obvious.

scriptText:
COCA-COLA × AMERICA250
"TOGETHER FOR 250" — AI ANIMATED TEASER
Production Script
Client: The Coca-Cola Company
Initiative: America250 Signature Partnership
Writer: StudioNow Creative
Date: 3/10/26
Version: 1
Format: :45–:60 AI-animated teaser
Genre: Experiential / Cinematic Teaser
Label: Internal Only | Confidential

CREATIVE BRIEF
This piece is not an explainer. It is a visual thesis.
The film should feel like memory in motion. AI is used to do what live action cannot: collapse time, blend eras, and turn history into a living, breathing experience. Coca-Cola is positioned as a constant cultural thread across 250 years of American life.

Visual Language: Painterly realism. Dimensional brush strokes. Organic textures. Slight imperfections (grain, bleed, softness). Environments morph into one another. No hard cuts where possible. Time behaves fluidly. Coca-Cola red is the connective visual thread.

One-Line Guidance: "We are painting memory, not recreating history. AI is the brush — humanity is the story."

SCRIPT
AUDIO / VO
SCENE / TC
VISUALS
SFX: Soft build of cinematic score begins. Warm strings, low hum. A single piano note rings.

VO (whisper, barely there):
"For generations... moments have brought us together."
SCENE 1
ORIGIN
0:00–0:08
A blank canvas. Warm sepia and Coca-Cola red bleed in like watercolor on wet paper.
Abstract early-America forms emerge through painterly brush strokes — roads stretching outward, hands reaching, movement westward. Dimensional textures, grain, organic imperfection.
A symbolic Coca-Cola bottle silhouette subtly forms from the landscape. No logo. Just the shape, like a memory surfacing.
AI Direction: Painterly realism. Sepia-to-red color migration. No hard edges.
SFX: Score deepens. Muted percussion enters. Strings swell with quiet pride.

VO:
"Through change, challenge, and celebration..."

Beat.

"Coca-Cola became part of America's story."
SCENE 2
HISTORY AS CULTURE
0:08–0:20
The world flows forward through time without cuts. Environments morph into one another like memories blending:
— A shared drink moment during hardship. Two anonymous, stylized figures passing a Coke across a table. Muted palette, Depression-era warmth.
— A community table in Atlanta. Color slowly saturates. Faces are implied, not rendered. No identifiable individuals.
— People from different eras passing a Coke hand to hand — a visual relay across decades. Brush strokes animate the transitions.
Coca-Cola red is the connective thread, pulling through every era.
AI Direction: Scenes melt into one another. Moving murals. Living posters. No hard cuts. Avoid hyper-real faces.
SFX: Music opens up. Brighter tones enter. A subtle beat drop as the map forms.

VO:
"250 years of a nation... always moving forward."

ALT VO:
"A quarter millennium. And every chapter shared."
SCENE 3
AMERICA250 EMERGES
0:20–0:32
The red thread expands outward, forming a stylized U.S. map. Painterly textures remain — this is not a clean graphic, it's a living canvas.
Communities light up across the country — cities, towns, coastlines glow with warm light. Each point pulses gently, like a heartbeat.
America250 iconography subtly forms from the landscape itself, emerging organically rather than overlaid.

SUPER: "America250 — Celebrating 250 Years"
(Minimal, elegant. Appears as if painted onto the canvas.)
SFX: Score lifts. Full rhythm section. Confident, modern, warm. Crowd energy subtly layered underneath.

VO:
"Now, as America looks ahead..."

"...we celebrate what unites us."
SCENE 4
THE PRESENT
0:32–0:44
Color palette brightens dramatically. Animation gains dimension and depth — the painterly style persists but with more vibrancy, more saturation.
Modern gatherings unfold in fluid succession:
— Music: a concert crowd, arms raised, Coca-Cola cups catching stage light
— Sport: a tailgate, ice-cold Cokes, communal energy
— Festivals: food trucks, color, motion, coolers, signage
Product appears naturally, never forced. Cups, coolers, the iconic contour shape — woven into the fabric of each moment.
AI Direction: Scenes continue to morph. Coca-Cola red remains the connective visual thread. Avoid stock-looking outputs.
SFX: Score reaches its peak. Hopeful crescendo. Then begins to soften, pulling back to intimate warmth.

VO:
"And imagine... what comes next."
SCENE 5
THE FUTURE
0:44–0:55
The map transforms into abstract future forms. The painterly world stretches forward:
— Youth: young faces implied through gesture and movement, not rendered in detail
— Creativity: color bursts, artistic energy, collaborative motion
— Innovation: fluid shapes suggesting technology without depicting screens or devices
— Community: hands connecting, circles forming, togetherness as visual motif
Coca-Cola red becomes a horizon line stretching forward — endless, warm, steady.
AI Direction: Peak visual complexity, then gradual simplification toward the lockup. The future is bright but not utopian — grounded, human.
SFX: Music resolves to a single sustained note. Soft cymbal swell. Silence.

VO (whisper):
"Together."
FINAL FRAME
LOCKUP
0:55–1:00
Motion resolves into stillness. The painterly world settles. Brush strokes slow, then stop.

Clean, elegant lockup fades in:

SUPER: "Coca-Cola × America250"
SUPER: "Together for 250"

Fade to black.

AI EXECUTION GUIDELINES
AI is a tool, not the subject. Human curation is essential throughout. Maintain a single emotional throughline from origin to lockup.
Acceptable Techniques: AI-generated keyframes with human animation, style-locked diffusion workflows, manual compositing for continuity control.
Avoid: Stock-looking outputs, hyper-real faces, photoreal CGI, literal historical recreation, tech-demo aesthetics.

DELIVERABLES
Final :45–:60 master
Style frames (if needed for pre-approval)
Layered project files (where applicable)

AI is a tool, not the subject. Human curation is essential throughout. Maintain a single emotional throughline from origin to lockup.

Final :45–:60 master

Adaptability: Script structure and VO are city-agnostic. Visual column contains [LOCALIZE] brackets indicating where city-specific restaurants, food, and b-roll swap in. One script, many versions.

LOCALIZATION GUIDE
Each city version requires:
1. City skyline/establishing shot (golden hour)
2. 3-4 local restaurant partners (non-chain, character-driven)
3. City-specific hero pairing shot (signature dish + Coke)
4. Closing city name graphic + evening cityscape
5. VO line "This is [CITY]" swapped per version

What stays the same across all versions:
VO script (except city name), music track, edit structure, pacing, Coca-Cola product shots, closing tagline and lockup.

SCRIPT
AUDIO / VO
SCENE / TC
VISUALS
SFX: City waking up. Distant traffic, birds, a coffee shop door opening. The hum of a place that knows how to eat.

MUSIC: Warm, soulful groove begins. Acoustic guitar + light percussion. Unhurried. Feels like a Saturday afternoon.
SCENE 1
:00–:08
WORLD-BUILDING

Establishing wide shot: [CITY SKYLINE]. Golden hour. The light is warm and generous.
Fast-cut montage of the city’s food identity: restaurant signs, kitchen windows, a hand-painted menu board, steam rising from a grill.

[LOCALIZE: Nashville = neon on Broadway + smoker exhaust. Atlanta = Buford Highway strip malls + peach-colored light. Chicago = L-train passing over a taqueria. Each city gets its own 3-shot skyline-to-street sequence.]
VO:
"Every city has its places."

"The ones you don’t find on a list... you find by following the smell."

SFX: Sizzle of a flat-top grill. A knife on a cutting board. Something frying in oil.
SCENE 2
:08–:18
THE RESTAURANTS

CU: Hands at work. A chef flipping something on a flat-top. Tongs pulling ribs. A tortilla hitting a hot comal.
We see 3-4 local restaurants in quick succession. Not chains. The places with character — the family photo on the wall, the hand-written specials, the line out the door.

[LOCALIZE: Feature 3-4 real local restaurants per city version. Nashville = hot chicken spot, meat-and-three, taco truck. Atlanta = soul food counter, Korean BBQ, seafood shack. Chicago = deep dish institution, jibarito spot, Maxwell Street grill.]

Coca-Cola appears naturally in every setting — a glass bottle on the counter, a fountain pour at the register, cans in a cooler. Present, never forced.
VO:
"The food tells you who lives here."

Beat.

"And the Coke... just makes sense."

SFX: Ice dropping into glass. Pour. Fizz rising. The satisfying crack of a cold can.
SCENE 3
:18–:30
THE PAIRING

This is the sensory heart of the spot. Slow-motion macro photography:
— A Coca-Cola poured over ice, condensation catching light
— CU of a bite and a sip, back to back. The rhythm of eating and drinking.
— A plate being set down next to a sweating glass bottle. The colors complement.
— Hands passing a Coke across a table at the same time as a shared plate.

Product hero shot: Coca-Cola glass bottle next to the city’s signature dish, shallow depth of field, restaurant atmosphere soft in the background.

[LOCALIZE: Hero pairing changes per city. Nashville = hot chicken + Coke. Atlanta = lemon pepper wings + Coke. Chicago = Italian beef + Coke. This is the thumbnail image for each version.]
VO:
"It’s not complicated."

"Good food. Cold Coke. The right people."

MUSIC: Groove lifts. Full rhythm section enters. Warm and confident. The vibe of a table where everybody’s talking.
SCENE 4
:30–:42
THE PEOPLE

We pull back from the food to the tables. The energy shifts from sensory to social:
— A family crowded into a booth, Cokes and plates everywhere. No one’s looking at a phone.
— Friends at an outdoor patio, laughing. A Coke gets passed across the table.
— A couple at a counter, splitting something messy. A shared Coke between them.
— A restaurant owner in the doorway, arms crossed, proud. Coca-Cola signage visible.

Fast-cut montage: clink of bottles, bites, laughter, napkins, the universal gestures of a meal enjoyed.

[LOCALIZE: Cast and locations reflect the demographic texture of each city. The restaurants should feel real because they are real.]
VO:
"This is [CITY]..."

"...and this is how we eat."

ALT VO:
"This is what [CITY] tastes like."

SFX: Street sounds return. Music pulls back. One final Coke pour, fizz fading.
SCENE 5
:42–:52
THE CITY AT NIGHT

Evening b-roll. The city at golden-to-blue hour. Restaurant lights glowing. A neon OPEN sign.
One final hero shot: a full table — food, Cokes, hands reaching. The frame holds.

SUPER: "[CITY NAME]"
(City name appears as a quiet, confident graphic. Not shouting. Placed.)

[LOCALIZE: City name swaps. Closing skyline/street shot is city-specific. Nashville = lit-up Lower Broad. Atlanta = Ponce City Market glow. Chicago = river walk at dusk.]
MUSIC: Final chord resolves. Warm. The kind of note that feels like pushing back from the table, satisfied.
FINAL
FRAME
:52–1:00
LOCKUP

SUPER: "Good Food. Cold Coke."

Coca-Cola logo lockup. Clean. Red on white.

ALT CLOSING SUPER:
"Every City. Every Table."

Fade to black.

PRODUCTION NOTES
Shoot Approach: One production crew per city. 1-2 day shoot per market. Focus on real restaurants during real service — capture the energy that’s already there, don’t manufacture it.
Product Integration: Coca-Cola appears in every scene but is never the subject of the shot. It’s the constant — the thing that’s always on the table. Glass bottles preferred for hero shots. Fountain and cans for environmental b-roll.
Talent: No actors. Real diners, real restaurant staff. If we need to direct moments, keep them simple: pour, sip, pass, clink. Nothing scripted.
Sound Design: Record live audio at each location. The sizzle, the chatter, the kitchen noise — these are as important as the music. Layer them under the score, not over it.
Scalability: This template can extend to any U.S. market. Each new city requires only the visual localization elements listed above. VO, music, edit, and pacing remain locked.

scriptText:
:30 COMMERCIAL
"CREDENTIALS OPTIONAL"
MR. PIBB IS BUILT DIFFERENT
Client: The Coca-Cola Company (Mr. Pibb)
Project: Spec Commercial
Writer: StudioNow Creative
Date: 3/10/26
Version: 1
Format: :30 brand film (AI-led production)
Genre: Experiential / Spec Commercial
Label: Internal Only | Confidential

CREATIVE BRIEF
Objective: Reframe Mr. Pibb not as a challenger competing on prestige, but as a soda that doesn’t need credentials to be enjoyed. Mr. Pibb isn’t anti-intellectual. It simply doesn’t worship status.
Key Message: MR. PIBB IS BUILT DIFFERENT. No advanced degree required to have a good time.
Tone: Dry, observational comedy. Deadpan delivery. Cinematic seriousness applied to simple moments. Confident, not chaotic. Funny because it’s restrained.
Comedy Approach: Comedy comes from contrast and timing, not jokes. Institutions overthink. Mr. Pibb does not. If it feels like sketch comedy, it’s wrong. If it feels calm but absurd, it’s right.
Visual Language: Clean, symmetrical compositions. Naturalistic but cinematic lighting. Shallow depth of field. Slight imperfections allowed and encouraged.

SCRIPT
AUDIO / SFX
TC
VISUALS
SFX: Quiet institutional hum. Low HVAC tone. The sound of a building that takes itself seriously.

MUSIC: Subtle classical cue begins. Stately. Measured. The kind of music that plays in a museum nobody visits.
:00–:03
SETUP
INT. DOCTOR’S OFFICE – DAY

Wide, symmetrical composition. An immaculately pristine doctor’s office. Clean lines, naturalistic but cinematic lighting, shallow depth of field.
The walls are absurdly covered in framed diplomas — Ivy League crests, Latin phrases, gold frames. Too many. Perfect. Overwhelming.

AI Direction: Static camera. Symmetrical frame. No human movement yet. Let the environment carry the comedy. Slight imperfections encouraged (dust motes, uneven light).
SFX: Heavy clipboard placed on desk. Dense thud of bureaucracy. Papers shift.
:03–:06
A doctor (50s, white coat) enters frame. Minimal movement — he functions as a symbol of the institution, not a character.
He places a thick clipboard on his desk. Dense paperwork, tabs, stamps, approvals.
Next to it sits a Mr. Pibb can.
The can feels... comfortable.

Performance: No dialogue. No expression. Face does not carry the comedy — framing does.
SFX: Frame nudges against wall. Soft click of glass settling. A second frame slips — barely audible.
:06–:09
CU: The doctor straightens one diploma, making it perfectly level.
Another diploma behind it slips slightly crooked.
He doesn’t notice.

AI Direction: Controlled camera motion. Slow push in. Comedy lives in the gap between precision and failure. No mugging.
SFX: Rapid-fire stamp hits. THUNK. THUNK. THUNK. Pause. Paper rustle. One more stamp.
:09–:12
WAITING ROOM
WAITING ROOM

A receptionist rapidly stamps paperwork. The stamp reads: APPROVED.
She pauses. Notices the Mr. Pibb can on the counter.
Considers it.
Stamps the paperwork again instead.

AI Direction: Medium shot, clean composition. Minimal facial performance. Object-led storytelling — the stamp and the can are the actors.
SFX: Pages turning. Thick paper. Medical journal thud as it closes.

SFX: CAN CRACK. Sharp, clean, satisfying. Fizz cuts through silence like a statement.
:12–:15
A patient sits quietly, flipping through a dense medical journal. Complex diagrams. Tiny text.
They close it.
Crack open Mr. Pibb.
The fizz cuts sharply through the silence.

AI Direction: The can crack is the punctuation mark of the entire spot. Give it room. Let the silence before it do the work.
SFX: Stethoscope on aluminum. A hollow, resonant tone. Fizz still audible inside the can.
:15–:18
OFFICE
Back in the office.
The doctor places a stethoscope against the Mr. Pibb can. Listens intently.
Nods once.
Makes a note on the clipboard.

AI Direction: Tight CU on stethoscope meeting can. Then cut to doctor’s face — deadpan. No smile. The comedy is that he’s completely serious.
SFX: Pen scratching on paper. Stops. Silence holds.
:18–:22
CU on clipboard.
Under “Assessment,” it simply reads:

NO NOTES.

The pen stops moving.

AI Direction: Hold this frame. Let it land. The handwriting should be imperfect, human. This is the emotional peak of the comedy.
MUSIC: Classical cue reaches its most assured moment. Not a crescendo — just a quiet arrival.
:22–:24
HERO SHOT
HERO SHOT

Mr. Pibb on the desk. Diplomas softly blurred behind. Shallow depth of field. The can is sharp, everything else is not.

SUPER: "MR. PIBB IS BUILT DIFFERENT"
(Centered. Clean. Confident typography.)
SFX: Clipboard set down. Small thud. Footsteps begin.
:24–:27
The doctor looks down at the clipboard. Overwritten. Overthought. Overprocessed.
He sets it down.
Looks at the Mr. Pibb.
A beat.
MUSIC: Score fades. Room tone returns. The building hums on without him.
:27–:30
LOCKUP
He casually takes the Mr. Pibb and exits frame, leaving the clipboard behind on the desk.
The office hum continues. Empty frame. The diplomas remain.

SUPER: "No advanced degree required to have a good time."

END.

AI PRODUCTION NOTES
Prioritize: Object-led storytelling. Institutional environments. Minimal human movement. Controlled camera motion.
Performance: Minimal acting. No dialogue required. Humans function as symbols of institutions (doctor, receptionist, patient). Faces do not carry the comedy — framing does. This avoids uncanny AI performance issues.
Sound Design: Quiet room tone. Subtle classical or institutional background music. Emphasize: can crack, fizz, paper stamps, small physical sounds. Sound carries comedy as much as visuals.
Avoid: Sketch comedy energy. Winking or mugging. Punchlines. Chaotic pacing. Flashy brand presence.

Wide, symmetrical composition. An immaculately pristine doctor’s office. Clean lines, naturalistic but cinematic lighting, shallow depth of field.

A doctor (50s, white coat) enters frame. Minimal movement — he functions as a symbol of the institution, not a character.
He places a thick clipboard on his desk. Dense paperwork, tabs, stamps, approvals.
Next to it sits a Mr. Pibb can.
The can feels... comfortable.

Another diploma behind it slips slightly crooked.
He doesn’t notice.

WAITING ROOM

Mr. Pibb on the desk. Diplomas softly blurred behind. Shallow depth of field. The can is sharp, everything else is not.

Looks at the Mr. Pibb.
A beat.
MUSIC: Score fades. Room tone returns. The building hums on without him.

LOCKUP
He casually takes the Mr. Pibb and exits frame, leaving the clipboard behind on the desk.
The office hum continues. Empty frame. The diplomas remain.
SUPER: "No advanced degree required to have a good time."

Prioritize: Object-led storytelling. Institutional environments. Minimal human movement. Controlled camera motion.

NAOU WOODRUFF CUFF SCRIPT

Date: 2/12/26
Version: 1

Case Title: NAOU | Woodruff Cup Submission 2025 Label: Internal Only | Confidential

AUDIO/VO
TC
VISUALS



VO:
January 1, 2025. The year began bubbling with optimism.
NAOU had delivered three consecutive years of growth. Qualified for Woodruff Cup every year since Emerging Stronger.
Strong plans across the portfolio. A fully aligned bottling system. An engaged team.
Momentum was real. And we were just getting started.


CHAPTER 1: MOMENTUM 
(0:00–0:25)

FOOTAGE:
	•	Sunrise over American landscapes. Blue skies. Fields. Iconic landmarks. Slow confident build.
	•	3-year revenue & operating income chart animates upward.
	•	Brand tiles appear across screen: Coke, BA, Fairlife, TM, Pibb.
	•	System Conference footage. Smiling teams. Bottlers shaking hands.
SUPERS:
	•	“3 Consecutive Years of Growth”
	•	“Qualified for Woodruff Cup — Every Year Since Emerging Stronger”
	•	“Fully Aligned Bottling System”
	•	“One Team”


out of nowhere… the perfect storm hit us.
A large-scale Hispanic boycott. Risk to our core trademark.
Our second largest bottler losing 30% of business.
An $800 million Fairlife investment off track.
A write-off on one of our biggest acquisitions.
Tariffs threatening billions in cost.
We finished Q1 behind plan.
The year… looked lost.

MUSIC/SFX:
Music shifts. Low percussion. Ominous tension.


CHAPTER 2: THE PERFECT STORM 

(0:25–0:55)



FOOTAGE:
	•	Sun flickers. Dark clouds roll in. Cut to stylized tsunami wave transition.=.
Smash cuts of headlines:
	•	Fake deportation video imagery (tasteful, not exploitative). Hispanic boycott headlines. (PROJECT TRUST)
	•	Cane sugar POV headlines.
	•	Reyes/Dr Pepper story.
	•	Fairlife plant halted.
	•	BodyArmor write-off press.
	•	Tariff headlines. Crushed Coke can.
SUPERS:
	•	“Volume Loss: XX MMUC” 
	•	Rev Loss: $YY
	•	“$800MM Investment at Risk” (placeholder)
	•	“$XB System Cost Exposure”
	•	“Q1 Behind Plan”

VO:
But we are paid to be winners. We are not paid to be victims.
VO:
We switched to Wartime CEO mode.
Driven by belief. Discipline. Learning.
One System. One Team.
---
CUT TO EMPLOYEE TESTIMONIALS IN STUDIONOW STUDIO
---














VO:
We moved with unprecedented speed.
Seven days to re-align Coke. Ninety days to relaunch Mr. Pibb. Weekly drumbeat on glass bottle. Continuous engagement with bottlers. Direct administration dialogue.
One system. Moving as one.


MUSIC/SFX:
Music lifts.



CHAPTER 3: WARTIME MODE (0:55–1:30)

VISUAL:
	•	Black and white photo of Roberto Goizueta.

	•	Cut to Jennifer Mann email (stylized screen graphic).


---
Daniel Langston
“We had seven days to replan Coke, and we did just that.”
Liz (Commercial)
“We re-launched Mr. Pibb in 90 days. I’ve never seen anything like it.”
Kristen (BA)
“We rebuilt BodyArmor with new identity and urgency.”
Andrew / Bottler
“Tariffs? We were scenario planning daily.”
Fairlife voice
“We got the plant back on track.”



VISUAL MONTAGE with VO:
	•	Coke “For Everyone” spot with 100m views on YouTube timelapse of all the views on Youtube.
	•	Mr. Pibb relaunch assets.
	•	BodyArmor new VIS.
	•	White House engagement moment.
	•	New glass bottle
	•	Cane sugar product release.
	•	One System Meeting Conference.
	•	Bottler handshake.
	•	Fairlife construction restart.



VO:
And then… the corner turned.
Coke For Everyone — 100 million views.
Trademark growth returned.
Mr. Pibb gained share.
Fairlife opened on schedule.
BodyArmor stabilized.
Full year metrics delivered.
(Insert stats from Maharaj Singh once received.)
MUSIC/SFX:
Music transitions to hopeful crescendo.


CHAPTER 4 – THE TURNAROUND 
(1:30–1:55)


VISUAL:
	•	Flower blooming from Coke bottle (metaphor).
	•	Revenue/OI graph turning upward.
	•	For Everyone YouTube view counter.
	•	Fairlife plant opening footage.
	•	Sports portfolio.
	•	Pibb shelf wins and visuals from the ad.
	•	Glass bottle in market.
	•	New Body Armor vis ID.
	•	System Conference applause.

SUPERS:
	•	“100MM+ Views”
	•	“Trademark Growth: X%”
	•	Share %, Revenue %, UC %
	•	“Mr. Pibb Share: +X”
	•	“Fairlife: On Schedule”
	•	“Delivered on All Woodruff Metrics” NEED DATA


VO (calm, earned)
Winning isn’t everything.
But the will to win is everything.
And this system… refuses to lose.

CLOSE (1:55–2:00)
Sun returns. Blue sky from Chapter 1.
System Conference group photo.


System Conference group photo. Opening slide of 3 year's being nominated, and add 4th year.

FINAL SUPERS
“Back. Stronger.”
 “There is no limit to what a man can do or where he can go if he doesn't mind who gets the credit". 
Fade out.

 Music: tight stutter build with effervescent/bubble textures. No VO.
 SFX: can pop → fizz hit.

:00-:12

THE PROBLEM TO SOLVE
VISUAL: 
UGC vertical quick-cuts (grimaces/taste-test reactions to “healthy sodas”).
 SUPERS/TOS (stacked, fast): “Healthy… but tastes fake?” → “Zero sugar… zero flavor?” 
“There has to be better.”
 – Pull 6–8 UGC taste-test clips from social exports 
Example HERE (4/10 rating)
 – If needed, use comment screenshots as cutaways.

 VO: “Then Coca-Cola did what no one expected: we made a soda that actually tastes amazing.”



12-:28

SIMPLY POP ARRIVES

Can hero + fizz + fruit macro. Slow pour.
 SUPER (lockup): Simply Pop — The Juicy New Soda
 Benefit Supers: “25–30% Real Fruit Juice • No Added Sugar • Prebiotic Fiber • Vitamin C + Zinc”

– Key product imagery/GIFs (Drive)
HERE & HERE

TASTE MEETS FUNCTION

teachingPoints: Partnership scripts need both sides to feel intentional, with the shared audience or cultural reason made visible.

scriptText:
NMA: MINUTE MAID: “BRING THE JUICE”
Date: 10/16//25
Version: 1
Case Title: Minute Maid x WWE: Bring the Juice Category: Retail Experience Label: Internal Only | Confidential
 Music:
	•	Intro / Outro: Kanye West – “POWER” (instrumental)
	•	Middle cue: Cody Rhodes “Fruit Punch” (Instagram reel clip)

AUDIO/VO
TC
VISUALS


AUDIO: Kanye West – POWER - Instrumental hits with slow build.

VO:
2024 — Minute Maid NAOU stood at the top of the juice world. #1 in Brand Power. The undisputed leader in juice. But… in a sea of sameness, being the best wasn’t enough. We needed to stand out. To make juice feel alive again.
ALT VO:
In 2024 Minute Maid NAOU was beginning a brand revolution, rising to be the #1 juice brand, the undisputed leader in Brand Power.

[0:00–0:10] – OPENING / TENSION
VISUAL: 
[Opening: WWE ring footage, quick smash-cuts with vibrant Minute Maid packaging, shoppers cheering.]
SUPER:
2024 — Minute Maid begins a Brand Revolution #1 Juice Brand • #1 in Brand Power



VO:
But… we had two big challenges: standing out from the crowd in a sea of juice — and proving we’re a modern, must-have brand.
 Only one thing left to do… Bring. The. Juice.




[0:10 – 0:25] THE CHALLENGE
VISUAL: Dull juice aisles fade to bright, energetic Minute Maid color palette.

Raw Video & Photos
SUPER:
Challenge: Stand out in a sea of sameness Goal: Make juice modern again


VO:
“Bring the Juice” wasn’t just a tagline. It was our rally cry — a product-centric, swagger-filled platform to turn life’s “meh” moments into vibrant wins.
 But we knew: to electrify this message we needed a partner as bold, confident, and culturally relevant as we are.

[0:25–0:40] IDEA:
 THE PLATFORM

 VISUAL: 
Close-up of bold packaging design 
→ slow-motion WWE superstars with dramatic lighting 
→ intercut with vibrant fruit imagery.


SUPER:
“Bring the Juice” — Our Rally Cry
 Swagger. Confidence. Energy.
SFX as each super appears for emphasis. 


AUDIO: Crowd pop / entrance music swell.
VO:
Enter WWE from the top rope, bringing endless energy, spectacle, and swagger straight into juice.
Transition to Hanumankind

[0:40–0:50] PARTNERSHIP: ENTER WWE

VISUAL: WWE arena lights, superstar walkouts, crowd reaction, dramatic logo reveal.

SUPER:
The Perfect Tag-Team Partner: WWE × Minute Maid
ASSETS: 
StudioNow MM Retail Sizzle Highlights


VO:
Together, we activated three knockout punches:
 One — Limited-edition Superstar Packaging across our Refreshments portfolio.
Punch SFX
 Two — Retail Theatre so loud and disruptive it turned stores into arenas.
Punch SFX
 Three — Electric storytelling woven into WWE’s biggest events: WrestleMania, Summer Slam, and even their $1B Netflix debut.
Punch SFX

[0:50–1:10] EXECUTION:

THREE KNOCKOUT PUNCHES
VISUAL: 

Quick-cut sequence of numbered hero moments.

SUPER:
① Superstar Packaging ② Retail Theatre → Stores Became Arenas ③ WWE Integration Across Marquee Events

ASSETS:

	•	Sizzle 1
	•	Raw Video & Photos

VO:
We deployed over 76,000 POS units — POS that was stealable… and sellable! Doubled pallet displays. Tripled shipper displays vs. other NAOU GOAT programs.
 Live retail appearances at 11 stores connected with more than 6,500 shoppers.

 [1:10–1:25] 
RETAIL EXPERIENCE / SCALE

VISUAL: Pallet displays shaped like rings, WWE + Minute Maid co-branding, shopper selfies, event signage.

SUPER:
76,000+ POS Units 2× Pallet Displays • 3× Shippers 11 Live Retail Events • 6,500+ Shoppers
MM_VIPSizzle_MIXED. | Powered by Box

VO:
The result? +10.5% revenue vs. year ago. DOUBLE that lift in activating retailers — +24%. #1 EQ share gainer across NAOU CCNA every month of the program: June, July, August 2025.
 We also recruited consumers to shop juice in ways we haven’t seen before.
 +1.9% household penetration, nearly $20M in value. 19% (805K households) were new to category.

[1:25–1:45] 

RESULTS:
PERFORMANCE IMPACT

VISUAL: 
Motion graphics of metrics overlaying crowd footage and product hero shots.
SUPER:
+10.5% Revenue vs YA +24% Activating Retailers #1 EQ Share Gainer (June–Aug) +1.9% HHP (+$20M) • 19% New to Category
ASSETS:
Sizzle 2
Freddy Video
AUDIO: Cody Rhodes “Fruit Punch” cue hits — upbeat, crowd vibe.
Cody Rhodes Instagram
VO:
We showed up in retail — with the highest SOVI in two years at 20.4%. Social engagement up +57% — proving people didn’t just see the juice… they felt it.

[1:45–1:55] 

CULTURAL & SOCIAL IMPACT


VISUAL: IG/TikTok clips, influencer moments, WWE stars sipping Minute Maid, fans cheering. 

SUPER:
+57% Social Engagement 20.4% Share of Visual Inventory (2-Year High) 

AUDIO: Final “POWER” sting… crowd chant builds to silence.
VO:
From retail… to the ring… to millions of impressions online… Minute Maid × WWE didn’t just Bring the Juice. 
We owned the moment. And the crowd went wild.

[1:55–2:00] 

CLOSING / TRANSFORMATION

SUPER (FINAL FRAME):
Minute Maid × WWE Bring the Juice. Retail Experience 2025

scriptText:
Global IMX: Custom Algorithm Script

Client: James Trott
Writer: Matthew Montemaro
Date: 6/2/25
Version: 1

Global IMX | “Custom Algorithm: The Future of Smarter Media”

AUDIO/VO
TC
VISUALS


Narrator (Voiceover):
 Today, most digital campaigns still follow the same rule — spend where the people are.
01:10
Fast-cut montage of cityscapes (Atlanta, Mexico City, Mumbai), marketing data charts populating, digital ad placements overlayed on population maps.
World map glowing with uniform ad spend overlays — generic, unfocused heatmaps. 

Narrator (Voiceover):

But what if we could rewrite the rules?

scriptText:
KOPIM SCRIPT

Client: Katie Dow
Date: 5/2/25
Version: 1

KOPIM | DIGITAL SHELF

AUDIO/VO
#
VISUALS



Narrator (Voiceover):
 "When consumers are faced with a choice, they expect and need the right information to inform their decision-making immediately, at their fingertips. Transparency, clarity, and accuracy are key. This is the power of KO PIM."

Can you provide some more options for this statement?
1
Scene 1: Consumer in Store Setting the Stage - Visual Introduction to Data Journey
 Open with a wide shot of a modern grocery store aisle. A consumer (young professional) walks down the aisle.
 The camera follows the consumer as they pause at a product, scanning the QR code on the packaging with their smartphone.
 On the phone screen, we see pop-ups of SmartLabel data, ingredients, marketing copy, and regulatory/legal requirements. These graphics pop on screen digitally around the consumer. 







Narrator (Voiceover):
 "The decision to purchase is made. Now, let’s follow the product through its data lifecycle."




2

 Cut to a close-up of the phone screen, highlighting the information available (SmartLabel, marketing copy, nutritional info, compliance details now on phone).
 The consumer smiles, nodding in satisfaction, and places the product in their basket.
 The camera pulls back to reveal the consumer walking to the checkout line.



Narrator (Voiceover):
 "From the checkout counter, the data doesn't stop. It travels through multiple systems, touching every step of the journey. This is where KO PIM, the backbone of our global data model, ensures everything is synchronized and streamlined."
3
Scene 2: Transition to Store Clerk - Data Ripple Effect Exploring Data Flow Backwards

 The consumer proceeds to the checkout counter.
 The cashier scans the barcode of the product.
 On-screen, we show a data “ripple” effect as we transition from in-store, zooming into the barcode information -- visually showcasing data as it travels along a journey from the store to the previous stages of the supply chain.


Narrator (Voiceover):
 "From the shelf back to the bottler, details such as product specifics, inventory, andforecasts.are connected by data elements from KO PIM. KO PIM collects, centralizes, and ensures that accurate data flows from our systems into the hands of bottlers and partners, driving  transactions."

5

 We transition to a delivery truck pulling into a bottler’s warehouse, unloading products.
 Data streams continue, showcasing decision making and bottlers in their workplace.

Narrator (Voiceover):
 "KO PIM isn’t just for the Digital Shelf it’s an enabler for brick-and-mortar operations and product commercialization too. By connecting all critical data, it powers tools like Workfront, KOArt, and KO Assets, ensuring that every piece of content, every piece of data, is governed and maintained with precision."
6
Scene 3: Bottler Receiving Data from KO PIM Explaining Bottler Integration and Data Flow
VISUAL:
	•	Show bottler b-roll (need assets) receiving and interacting with the product data at their warehouse (product packaging, inventory management systems, etc.).

	•	Show screen recording of how bottlers use KO PIM to access  product data and imagery, Include icons or interface visuals of Workfront, KOArt, and KO Assets.

Narrator (Voiceover):
 "Data governance is the heart of KO PIM. Clear data ownership, dedicated teams of analysts and stewards ensure the integrity of product data, empowering teams to manage the product lifecycle from creation to the shelf—and beyond."
7
  Show the different KO PIM-connected tools (Workfront, KOArt, KO Assets, ISSCOM, KOmply, KO Link) functioning in sync, with data being updated in real time across the different platforms.
  The interface screens of these tools smoothly transition into each other, showing data flowing back into the system.

Narrator (Voiceover):
 "KO PIM’s data governance not only supports bottlers and brick-and-mortar but also drives the ecomm experience.  Complete and accurate product  data combined with digital assets ensures a consistent message for  consumers interacting with our products online. This drives informed purchasing decisions, AI-driven recommendations, and data analytics."


8
Scene 4: Fast Forward to Ecomm and Digital Shelf Connecting to Ecomm and Digital Experience
VISUAL:
	•	Show digital assets, product images, and marketing copy being uploaded onto consumer-facing platforms like Brands.com, customer portals, and Digital Shelf.
	•	Product images appear on-screen, then transition into a product page with clear data (e.g., product description, nutrition facts, SmartLabel, etc.).
Narrator (Voiceover):
 "From product inception to checkout, KO PIM connects, enables, and powers every step of the product lifecycle. This is the future of data, the future of products, and the future of consumer experiences."
10
  Close-up of a digital product page showing rich product data (e.g., a high-quality image, detailed descriptions, marketing copy, and customer reviews).
  We transition back to the in-store QR scan and online checkout, tying it all together with seamless data flow across platforms, as we pan up to the customer smiling and walking to exit the store.

Narrator (Voiceover):
 "KO PIM—Centralized. Governed. Empowered. Enabling the future of product data."
11
Scene 5: Closing Shot and Call to Action End on KO PIM Branding
VISUAL:
	•	The KO PIM logo appears on screen.
	•	Tagline: "KO PIM—Centralized. Governed. Empowered. Enabling the future of product data."


13
TCCC | KOPIM | DIGITAL SHELF LOGOS

Partnership scripts need both sides to feel intentional, with the shared audience or cultural reason made visible.
Platform explainers need a human friction first, then a clean system reveal and proof of usefulness.
Sizzles depend on sharp supers, escalation, and visual momentum more than heavy VO.
Supers should carry argument beats, not label the obvious.

scriptText:
2025 THINK STRATEGIC SCRIPT

Date: 8/15/25
Version: 1

Think Strategic: Courage over Comfort	

AUDIO/VO
TC
VISUALS



VO: “Welcome to Nashville. Welcome to the 2025 Think Strategic Summit.” 
 SFX: Soft build of cinematic, inspiring score begins (warm strings + muted percussion).

:00-:06
VISUAL: Slow-motion aerial drone shots of Nashville skyline at sunrise; light spilling over Cumberland River.


VO: “For the next two and a half days, we unite the brightest minds in our FSOP community — leaders who shape, define, and disrupt the foodservice industry.”



07-:18



Fast-paced montage: hotel exterior (Four Seasons Nashville), bustling streets, Wide generic silhouettes of executives in conversation (stock footage). 

VO: “We gather at a time when the economic landscape is shifting beneath our feet. Growth rates temper. Costs rise. Consumer behaviors evolve at lightning speed.”

:19-:32

Economic graphics animate in — pulled from “Economy at a Glance” slides — line graphs, inflation headlines, industry stats layered over stylized b-roll of busy restaurants, drive-thrus, and kitchen operations.


VO: “In this climate, courage isn’t optional. It’s essential. Our theme — Courage over Comfort — is a call to lead boldly, to innovate relentlessly, and to reimagine what’s possible.”
:33-:43
Strong hero shots: leaders in discussion, close-up hands sketching ideas, coffee cups hitting tables. Graphic lockup: “Courage over Comfort.”

VO: “This week, you’ll be challenged to think differently, to collaborate deeply, and to explore ideas that spark real transformation.

:44-:56
Diverse faces in workshops, sticky-note brainstorms, animated design thinking flow diagrams subtly integrated.


VO: “We’ll take you inside The Vault — Coca-Cola’s Collaboration Center — where innovation meets partnership. A place designed for ideation, co-creation, and building solutions side by side with our customers.”

:57-1:09

Footage from Vault deck: modern interiors, interactive screens, product innovation zones, teams presenting concepts together.
VO: “And we’re joined by voices that inspire: Jesse Cole, the visionary behind the Savannah Bananas… and Olympic champion Laurie Hernandez.”
1:10-1:21
Sports highlight stock resembling Savannah Bananas energy (crowd cheering, yellow uniforms — no unlicensed logos) and gymnast training/performance clips.

VO: “Over these days, you’ll gain the insights, tools, and relationships to write your 2026 roadmap — together.”
1:22-1:36

Animated roadmap graphic transitioning to stock footage of strategy meetings, digital dashboards, and cityscapes.

VO: “This is more than a summit. It’s a spark. A space where courage meets opportunity… and the future takes shape.” 
 SFX: Score swells, adding percussion drive.
1:37-1:54

Cinematic montage of Nashville’s cultural icons — Broadway lights, live music close-ups, artisans at work — blended with abstract Coca-Cola red accents in motion graphics.

VO: “Welcome to Nashville. Welcome to Think Strategic 2025.”  SFX: Music hits final chord with a soft cymbal swell.

1:55-2:00
Logo lockup: Coca-Cola + Think Strategic Summit branding; “Courage Over Comfort” tagline; fade to black.

StudioNow to produce (1) video highlighting the importance of KOPIM for internal usage. Overall Approach: The KO PIM video will showcase the journey of a product from the consumer's in-store purchase to its data journey across the entire supply chain, highlighting how KO PIM enables seamless data flow and governance. We will emphasize KO PIM's role not just in ecomm but as an essential tool for brick-and-mortar operations, bottlers, and data-driven systems like Workfront and KOArt, ensuring accurate and synchronized product information from creation to the consumer experience. Scope includes:
	•	Executive Creative Director
	•	Creative Producer
	•	Production Crew for (1) Day Shoot
	•	DP, HMU, Wardrobe, Lighting Tech, Camera Gear + Accessories
	•	Talent for (1) Day Shoot
	•	Video Editor
	•	Motion Graphics
	•	Music
	•	Sound Design
Prelim Production Details: 3k Sarah Wissenback (w/ phone comp) $800 John Roberts audio 2k Bernal (director + storyboard/shotlist prep) + gear to supplement CCCS if nec. 1k DP $1,200 Audio $750/800 Gaffer Bill Marinella for cast (extra) CCCS FX6 // Lens CCCS Lighting gear Leah PA Location: Gratis, Quiktrip - where Rick shot, or Day in the Life. ~9k casting Extra - $300 + 20% = $360 · Bill for casting  VFX - post  Graphics - pre-made assets to show on phone  Art · prop phone Intake Call Notes  Creative:
	•	KO PIM Narrative:
	•	getting lost in the digital shelf shuffle
	•	new markets only associate KO PIM with e-commerce, need to associate with brick+mortar, global data models for product data
	•	help people understand full purpose of KOPIM
	•	Launched in March 2024 , 13 markets onboard , dont want to lose momentum
	•	Need to tie into digital shelf -but approach from the full scale of KOPIM
	•	Video
	•	CUSTOMER + POS: Starts at POS with customer, they scan barcode and they learn ALL about the product (i.e. product, nutrition, price, etc) all data fueled by KOPIM
	•	CHECKOUT: Clerk scans and all this info comes up from KOPIM
	•	Start at consumer and work backwards to how product originiates. End of Digital Shelf.
	•	AI Vs. Standard Production/Film Options
	•	Should be able to standalone but also showcased with Digital Shelf Video
	•	Creative Inspo:
	•	LOVE KOart video - but be more global in the approach for KOPIM
	•	Assets:
	•	Grip has renders
	•	Audience:
	•	Bottlers on roadshows to see new capabilities
	•	Internal TCCC Teams for Workfront integrations - why is it valuable for them on project data
	•	External TCCC Partners /not consumer facing
	•	Tone:
	•	tech-forward + uplifting/comrardery to show partnership with bottlers on the data piece

	•	Aim for earlier
scriptText:
KOPIM SCRIPT

Talent: Heidi Rew
Date: 8/1/25
Producer: Mike Karam

#
VOICEOVER

1
Refreshing the world is a big task and one that depends on data. Coca-Cola’s Product Information Management system, or KO PIM is the heart of product data.
Just as retailers depend on reliable product information to stock their shelves with confidence, consumers faced with a choice expect that same accurate information at their fingertips immediately. Transparency, clarity and accuracy help turn browsers into buyers.

2
KO PIM empowers not only the digital shelf, but also the physical shelf, supply chain, and… consumer choices.

3
Let’s trace the data backwards to see how KO PIM collects, harmonizes, and ensures the consistency of product data and imagery – all in one streamlined process.

4
Before the consumer purchasing decision, our bottlers leverage product details from KO PIM such as weights, dimensions, and brand information. 

5
KO PIM is not only an enablement tool for the Shelf, but also for other product innovation platforms such as Workfront, KOArt, and KO Assets, ensuring that every piece of content – and every piece of data -  is governed and maintained with precision. 

6
Data governance is fundamental to KO PIM. 

Dedicated teams of analysts and stewards take on data ownership to foster integrity and trust in our data.

7
KO PIM provides future opportunities such as language translations on the fly, automated content generation, AI-driven recommendations, and advanced data analytics.

8
From product innovation to checkout, KO PIM connects, enables, and powers every step of the product lifecycle. This is the future of data, the future of products, and the future of consumer experiences.

9
KO PIM—Centralized. Governed. Empowered.

notes: Internal platform-explainer sizzle for Coca-Cola's KO PIM product-data system. Reverse-narrative: starts at the consumer point of sale and walks backwards through the supply chain to show how product data flows. Audience: bottlers on roadshows, internal TCCC teams, external TCCC partners (not consumer-facing).
teachingPoints: Reverse-narrative structure (POS → back to origin) makes an internal-systems story feel like a journey.
Tech-forward tone balanced with uplifting/camaraderie tone for bottler-partnership angle.
VO numbered by beat (1, 2, 3...) for easy revision tracking and music-cue alignment.
Treats data governance and supply-chain plumbing as a brand value, not a feature list.
Anchors abstract product-data concepts to concrete consumer behavior (scanning a barcode in store).

notes: BODYARMOR section of the Advanced Hydration umbrella brief (vitaminwater + smartwater + Powerade + BodyArmor). Final shipped sizzle for RTS 2027. Tight TOS-only structure: SUPER paired with VISUALS line-by-line. Heavy on partnership references (NASCAR, March Madness, Savannah Bananas, Dude Perfect) and product platform names (Base, Lyte, Flash IV, FIT).
teachingPoints: Tight SUPER+VISUALS pairing line-by-line is the StudioNow house structure for text-on-screen sizzles — no extra prose between rows.
Use named real partnerships (Savannah Bananas, NASCAR, Dude Perfect) as proof points instead of generic 'cultural moments' language.
Open with a one-line goal frame ('IN 2027, OUR GOAL IS SIMPLE') before listing tactics.
Stack tactics in numbered structural buckets ('FIRST...', 'SECOND...') so the read survives at LED-wall scale.
For umbrella briefs covering multiple brands, lift the brand-specific section into a self-contained sizzle that does not depend on the other brand sections to make sense.

Metrics work as supers when each gets its own beat and the visual amplifies (motion, product) rather than repeats the number.

For a :30, six beats of 4-6 seconds each is the ceiling — one idea per beat.

notes: Final V3 of the :60 Advanced Hydration portfolio sizzle covering four brands from one umbrella bumper brief. Cold open: black screen, one silent beat, hard music drop. Time-coded at 2-second granularity. Universal-behavior tension open, then brand-by-brand chapters.
teachingPoints: Cold-open device: black screen and one beat of silence before a hard music drop buys more attention than any opening image.
Open portfolio pieces on a universal behavior, then counterpoint with how this portfolio does it differently.
Time-code at 2-second granularity for fast TOS cutting — each super lands on a music hit.
Give each portfolio brand a chapter with its own micro-arc instead of interleaving brands.
State format, tone, and track in a header block so production reads the intent before the first beat.
